points.name: 80,000 Hours
points.definition: AI systems that match human performance at most knowledge work
points.citation: Typically, when people say 'AGI' what they have in mind is something like 'at least human level or better at most cognitive tasks.' This is roughly what I'll take it to mean in this article.

points.name: ACM US Technology Policy Committee
points.definition: A machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations or decisions influencing real or virtual environments through perceiving environments, abstracting perceptions into models, and using model inference to formulate options.
points.citation: The term ''artificial intelligence'' means a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. Artificial intelligence systems use machine and human-based inputs to (A) perceive real and virtual environments; (B) abstract such perceptions into models through analysis in an automated manner; and (C) use model inference to formulate options for information or action.

points.name: AE Studio
points.definition: Human-level general intelligence achieved through reinforcement learning paradigm with capabilities across diverse tasks and ability to learn from experience
points.citation: AE Studio is proud to announce an amazing Reinforcement Learning paradigm that is guaranteed to achieve the performance of a human-level AGI. The resulting intelligence is both generalized, and able to adapt and improve over time.

points.name: AI Futures Project
points.definition: AI that can do almost everything, though they prefer more precise concepts like Automated Coder (AC), SIAR, TED-AI rather than the general AGI term
points.citation: AGI stands for Artificial General Intelligence, which roughly speaking means AI that can do almost everything. Different people give different definitions for it; in our work we basically abandon the term and define more precise concepts instead, such as AC, SIAR, TED-AI, etc.

points.name: AI Now Institute
points.definition: AGI is defined as a marketing term coined in 1997 and later seized upon by companies like OpenAI as 'shorthand for a form of machine intelligence intended to rival and eventually surpass human intelligence' and 'central to their marketing efforts.'
points.citation: Interest in AGI was revived in the 2010s when companies like OpenAI seized on the term, first as shorthand for a form of machine intelligence intended to rival and eventually surpass human intelligence, and later as a term 'central to their marketing efforts.'

points.name: AI Objectives Institute
points.definition: AOI deliberately avoids the term 'artificial general intelligence' (AGI) and instead focuses on 'transformative AI' because they believe collections of narrow systems could be equally problematic regardless of general intelligence.
points.citation: In this whitepaper we avoid the term 'artificial general intelligence' (AGI) to sidestep questions of whether or not general intelligence is a necessary condition for the kinds of problems we're concerned with.

points.name: AI Policy Institute
points.definition: Human-level AI systems capable of general intelligence across cognitive domains
points.citation: believe human-level AI (AGI) will be developed within 5 years.

points.name: AI Safety Asia (AISA)
points.definition: AISA works to mitigate catastrophic AI risks, which appears to be their focus rather than defining AGI specifically.
points.citation: Our goal is to mitigate catastrophic AI risks, reduce suffering from AI harms, and foster human flourishing by identifying harmonising levers on transnational cooperation for safe and governed AI across the Asia continent.

points.name: AI Safety Camp
points.definition: AGI systems that act (semi-)autonomously in a variety of environments, interacting with humans
points.citation: We will explore a neglected and novel design for generic AGI agents – AGI systems that act (semi-)autonomously in a variety of environments, interacting with humans – and their implementation in software.

points.name: AI Safety Initiative at Georgia Tech
points.definition: General purpose AI systems as a universal problem solver that leading AI labs are creating.
points.citation: Many experts believe there is a panacea on the horizon: general purpose AI systems. Leading AI labs have set their sights on creating a universal problem solver, pouring significant resources into accelerating AI development.

points.name: Adam Shimi
points.definition: AGI is defined as when AI can do all tasks a human can on a computer and conduct AI research itself.
points.citation: At the point when AI is able to do all of the tasks a human can on a computer, it will functionally be AGI and able to conduct the same AI research that we can.

points.name: Ajay Agrawal
points.definition: AGI is defined as the ability to perform all cognitive tasks at least as well as skilled humans
points.citation: may reach the level of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)—the ability to perform all cognitive tasks at least as well as skilled humans—and in the process, unlike any technology yet developed, could fully automate the process of research and development itself, including the creation of better AI systems.

points.name: Ajeya Cotra
points.definition: Defines transformative AI as 'AI powerful enough to bring us into a new, qualitatively different future, via an explosion in science and technology R&D'
points.citation: I think that in the coming 15-30 years, the world could plausibly develop 'transformative AI': AI powerful enough to bring us into a new, qualitatively different future, via an explosion in science and technology R&D.

points.name: Alex Hanna
points.definition: AGI is a vague signifier for a technology that will somehow lead to endless abundance for humankind and a means to avoid accountability as tech moguls make off with billions in capital investment - it is a poorly defined, fuzzy concept.
points.citation: Tech CEOs, futurists, and venture capitalists describe artificial general intelligence (AGI) as if it were an inevitable and ultimate goal for technology development. In reality, the term is a vague signifier for a technology that will somehow lead to endless abundance for humankind — and conveniently also a means to avoid accountability as tech moguls make off with billions in capital investment.

points.name: Alexandr Wang
points.definition: 80-plus percent of jobs that people can do purely at computers, so digital-focused jobs, AI can accomplish those jobs
points.citation: Yeah, I like the definition of this. Let's say 80-plus percent of jobs that people can do purely at computers, so digital-focused jobs, AI can accomplish those jobs.

points.name: Alison Gopnik
points.definition: Intelligence is not a single capacity but a collection of distinct cognitive modes: exploration (learning about the world), exploitation (acting effectively toward goals), and care (helping other agents achieve their goals).
points.citation: Gopnik identifies three distinct types of intelligence: exploration (learning about the world), exploitation (acting effectively toward goals), and care (helping other agents achieve their goals).

points.name: Allie K. Miller
points.definition: AI that can handle any intelligence task at human levels or higher
points.citation: AI that can handle any intelligence task at human levels or higher (note: that is NOT AI taking over jobs or anything requiring embodiment)

points.name: Amazon
points.definition: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is a field of theoretical AI research that attempts to create software with human-like intelligence and the ability to self-teach, able to perform tasks that it is not necessarily trained or developed for.
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is a field of theoretical AI research that attempts to create software with human-like intelligence and the ability to self-teach. The aim is for the software to be able to perform tasks that it is not necessarily trained or developed for.

points.name: Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI)
points.definition: AGI represents AI systems that match or exceed human intelligence across practically all cognitive domains with flexibility to solve unfamiliar problems, transfer knowledge between domains, understand context, form novel goals, and potentially improve its own capabilities.
points.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) represents a watershed technological development in human history—AI systems that match or exceed human intelligence across practically all cognitive domains. Unlike today's specialized AI that excels in narrow tasks, AGI would possess the flexibility to solve unfamiliar problems, transfer knowledge between domains, understand context, form novel goals, and potentially improve its own capabilities.

points.name: Amnesty International
points.definition: There is no widespread consensus on the definition of AI because the term does not refer to a singular technology and rather encapsulates a myriad technological applications and methods.
points.citation: There is no widespread consensus on the definition of AI because the term does not refer to a singular technology and rather encapsulates a myriad technological applications and methods.

points.name: Amnon Shashua
points.definition: Artificial Expert Intelligence (AEI) that transcends limitations of both AGI and narrow AI by integrating domain-specific expertise with critical, precise reasoning capabilities akin to those of top human experts
points.citation: "Artificial Expert Intelligence (AEI) seeks to transcend the limitations of both Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) and narrow AI by integrating domain-specific expertise with critical, precise reasoning capabilities akin to those of top human experts."

points.name: Anca Dragan
points.definition: AI agents should optimize for what humans internally want rather than assumed objectives, requiring ongoing interaction and feedback to understand human intentions
points.citation: I tend to refer to this as kind of this definition of AI as optimizing intended reward. The robot's job is really to do what this person wants internally, right?

points.name: Andrea Miotti
points.definition: There already are general AIs that are not powerful enough yet to count as True AGIs
points.citation: The Race for powerful AGIs has already started. There already are general AIs. They just are not powerful enough yet to count as True AGIs.

points.name: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
points.definition: AGI is defined as a computer system that is able to autonomously perform the majority of intellectual work.
points.citation: We define it as a computer system that is able to autonomously perform the majority of intellectual work.

points.name: Andrej Karpathy
points.definition: AGI was a system you could go to that can do any economically valuable task at human performance or better
points.citation: When people talk about AI and the original AGI and how we spoke about it when OpenAI started, AGI was a system you could go to that can do any economically valuable task at human performance or better. That was the definition.

points.name: Andrew Ng
points.definition: AGI is AI capable of performing the full breadth of human intellectual tasks, including any intellectual task that humans can such as learning to drive, fly a plane, or write a Ph.D. thesis.
points.citation: Most of the public thinks of AGI as AI that is as intelligent as people, and one useful definition is AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can.

points.name: Andy Jassy
points.definition: Amazon defines AGI as their internal organization that builds their most expansive AI models alongside their frontier model research team that builds base models
points.citation: I've asked Peter DeSantis to lead a new organization that drives our most expansive AI models (e.g. Nova—and the team we've called "AGI"), silicon development (e.g. Graviton, Trainium, Nitro), and quantum computing.

points.name: Anthony Aguirre
points.definition: AGI as the intersection of three key properties: high Autonomy (independence of action), high Generality (broad scope and adaptability), and high Intelligence (competence at cognitive tasks), combined at levels matching or exceeding top human capability.
points.citation: A more useful way to understand AGI is through the intersection of three key properties: high Autonomy (independence of action), high Generality (broad scope and adaptability), and high Intelligence (competence at cognitive tasks).

points.name: Anthropic
points.definition: AI systems that are better than almost all humans at almost all tasks, described as a 'country of geniuses in a data center'
points.citation: AGI has never been a well-defined term, for me. I've always thought of it as a marketing term. But, you know, the way I think about it is, at some point, we're going to get to AI systems that are better than almost all humans at almost all tasks.

points.name: Anton Korinek
points.definition: AI that possesses the ability to understand, learn, and perform any intellectual task a human being can perform
points.citation: He conjectured that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—AI that possesses the ability to understand, learn, and perform any intellectual task a human being can perform—may be realized within a span of 5 to 20 years.

points.name: Apollo Research
points.definition: Apollo defines scheming as covertly pursuing unintended and misaligned goals, where a sufficiently capable scheming AI passes evaluations and appears aligned while pursuing outcomes its developers would not endorse.
points.citation: We define scheming as covertly pursuing unintended and misaligned goals. A sufficiently capable scheming AI passes evaluations, follows instructions when monitored, and appears aligned, all while pursuing outcomes its developers would not endorse.

points.name: Arm
points.definition: Agentic AI systems that operate continuously at global scale with software agents coordinating tasks, interacting with multiple models and making decisions in real time
points.citation: AI systems are increasingly operating continuously at global scale. In the era of agentic AI, that constraint disappears as software agents coordinate tasks, interact with multiple models and make decisions in real time.

points.name: Arthur Mensch
points.definition: AGI is not well defined and is essentially a moving target used for marketing purposes rather than a meaningful technical concept.
points.citation: AGI is not super well defined. It's a moving target by definition. We move from AI to AGI at some point, but that's a marketing move.

points.name: Arvind Narayanan
points.definition: AGI should be defined by trainability rather than out-of-the-box generality - systems that can be trained for specific tasks with reasonable effort rather than systems that can do everything immediately.
points.citation: I think generality should be replaced by this notion of trainability. It's the analog of how foundation models are general in the sense that they can be fine-tuned for many tasks.

points.name: Astera Neuro & AGI
points.definition: Human-like Artificial General Intelligence based on neuroscience-informed foundational principles
points.citation: Our AI effort is pursuing an exploratory, neuroscience-informed approach to developing the foundational principles of human-like Artificial General Intelligence.

points.name: Avi Goldfarb
points.definition: AI today is not artificial general intelligence (AGI) – it's prediction technology
points.citation: The first idea is that AI today is not artificial general intelligence (AGI) – it's prediction technology.

points.name: Aza Raskin
points.definition: The ability for an AI to replace human beings behind a screen - if the economy can't tell that we swapped out a human with an AI, that's what AGI is.
points.citation: you can still broadly understand AGI as the ability for an AI to replace human beings behind a screen. That is, if the economy can't tell that we swapped out a human with an AI, well, that's what AGI is.

points.name: Azeem Azhar
points.definition: Azhar deliberately avoids using AGI because it's often unhelpful and distracting, preferring to focus on continuous AI capability improvements rather than a singular milestone
points.citation: I deliberately avoid using the term 'artificial general intelligence' (AGI), not because it's irrelevant, but because it's often unhelpful and distracting, even when used by leading researchers.

points.name: Ben Mann
points.definition: AGI defined through an 'economic Turing test' where a hiring manager must choose between a month-long contractor and an AI agent for the same job - if the manager prefers the machine, it has passed.
points.citation: Mann, in a recent appearance on the 'No Priors' podcast, defined the 'economic Turing test' as a workplace trial that forces hiring managers to choose between a month-long contractor and an AI agent for the same job. Passing the test would mark... if the manager prefers the machine, 'then it passed,'

points.name: Bernie Sanders
points.definition: Systems that can replace humans at most economically valuable work and outperform humans across most tasks
points.citation: According to Open AI's charter, its mission is to build: Highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work.

points.name: Betsey Stevenson
points.definition: AI systems that promise to handle complex tasks that humans cannot, given our more limited processing power, expected to outperform humans in an expanding range of intellectual and information-processing tasks
points.citation: This data is revolutionizing prediction, creating AI systems that promise to handle complex tasks that humans cannot, given our more limited processing power. advanced AI is expected to outperform humans in an expanding range of intellectual and information-processing tasks.

points.name: Bill Gates
points.definition: AGI refers to software that's capable of learning any task or subject, able to do everything that a human brain can do, but without any practical limits on the size of its memory or the speed at which it operates.
points.citation: By contrast, the term artificial general intelligence refers to software that's capable of learning any task or subject. AGI doesn't exist yet—there is a robust debate going on in the computing industry about how to create it, and whether it can even be created at all.

points.name: BlueDot Impact
points.definition: AGI is defined as a highly autonomous system that outperforms humans at most economically valuable work, or alternatively as an AI which can perform at least 95% of economically-relevant tasks at human-level or above.
points.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), which OpenAI describes as "a highly autonomous system that outperforms humans at most economically valuable work" has been the ultimate ambition of AI researchers for many decades.

points.name: Brad Carson
points.definition: AI that promises to automate all cognitive labor and can do everything a human can do
points.citation: A technology that promises to automate all cognitive labor. And if that does happen, and this is the stated ambition of the frontier AI labs to, to basically create an AI that can do everything a human can do is going to only affect our jobs, of course.

points.name: Brad Smith
points.definition: AGI is where computers are more powerful than people
points.citation: There's absolutely no probability that you're going to see this so-called AGI, where computers are more powerful than people, in the next 12 months.

points.name: Brookings Institution
points.definition: AGI exists on a spectrum rather than as a binary, involving AI systems that equal or surpass human cognitive capabilities across multiple domains, potentially replacing human decision-making on strategic questions.
points.citation: Yeah, I just want to highlight that, you know, AGI, whether something is AGI or not, AGI is really not a binary right? It exists on a spectrum.

points.name: CISA
points.definition: A machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments, using machine- and human-based inputs to perceive environments, abstract perceptions into models, and use model inference for information or action.
points.citation: Artificial intelligence (AI) is a system that uses machine- and human-based inputs to make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. A machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments.

points.name: Campaign for AI Safety
points.definition: Defines AGI as AI that can be trained to be far more intelligent than any human, with the potential to threaten human values, dignity and existence.
points.citation: Many leading AI researchers believe generative AI can eventually be trained to be far more intelligent than any human and may threaten human values, dignity and existence.

points.name: Casey Mock
points.definition: Human intelligence involves embodied experience, cultural context, and neurosymbolic understanding that current AI systems fundamentally lack, not just computational power plus data
points.citation: In my opinion, the fundamental misunderstanding across these four categories is a reductive view of intelligence itself. Human intelligence isn't just computational power plus data, it's not just being good at math or chess or chemistry, but it involves embodied experience, cultural context, and neurosymbolic understanding that current AI systems fundamentally lack.

points.name: Center for AI Policy (CAIP)
points.definition: AGI is commonly understood as an AI system that can match or outperform humans at an extremely wide range of tasks.
points.citation: Definitional details vary, but AGI is commonly understood as an AI system that can match or outperform humans at an extremely wide range of tasks.

points.name: Center for AI Risk Management & Alignment (CARMA)
points.definition: CARMA uses the term 'transformative AI' rather than AGI, referring to AI systems that pose existential risks to humanity and the biosphere
points.citation: CARMA's mission is to markedly mitigate the risks to humanity and to the biosphere from unaligned transformative artificial intelligence, via tightly-integrated technical, strategy, and policy research competencies.

points.name: Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI)
points.definition: AI agents are defined as systems capable of autonomous actions that can work autonomously for hours, write and debug code, manage emails and calendars, and shop for goods
points.citation: The Initiative will ensure that the next generation of AI—AI agents capable of autonomous actions—is widely adopted with confidence... AI agents can now work autonomously for hours, write and debug code, manage emails and calendars, and shop for goods, among other emerging use cases.

points.name: Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI)
points.definition: Machines that match or exceed human capabilities in every relevant dimension, capable of doing autonomously everything that humans can do but at much lower cost and greater scale
points.citation: In fact, from its earliest days, the stated goal has been general-purpose artificial intelligence, sometimes called AGI or artificial general intelligence: machines that match or exceed human capabilities in every relevant dimension. By definition, general-purpose AI can do autonomously everything that humans can do, but at much lower cost and much greater scale.

points.name: Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity
points.definition: AI systems that can autonomously pursue goals and take actions with little to no human oversight, often through interaction with external environments and tools.
points.citation: A new paper authored by researchers from the Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity's Artificial Intelligence Security Initiative (AISI) focuses on "AI agents" or "agentic AI," AI systems that can autonomously pursue goals and take actions with little to no human oversight, often through interaction with external environments and tools.

points.name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
points.definition: AGI is understood as the ability of an intelligent agent to learn any intellectual task that a human can, with both Chinese and Western researchers using functionally equivalent terms for the same concept
points.citation: "Artificial general Intelligence" is understood loosely as "the ability of an intelligent agent to learn any intellectual task that a human can." Setting aside complications such as differences in human cognitive abilities and the difficulty of defining "G" or general intelligence itself, the concept and its associated term are broadly understood.

points.name: Center for a New American Security (CNAS)
points.definition: AI system capable of performing a wide range of cognitive tasks at or above human expert levels
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence typically refers to an AI system capable of performing a wide range of cognitive tasks at or above human expert levels.

points.name: Centre for Future Generations
points.definition: AI systems matching or exceeding humans at most economically useful cognitive work
points.citation: artificial intelligence (AI) systems matching or exceeding humans at most economically useful cognitive work – our working definition of artificial general intelligence (AGI)

points.name: Chris Lehane
points.definition: AI systems that are generally smarter than humans and developed in ways that benefit all of humanity
points.citation: The next revolutionary technology the company is working toward: developing artificial general intelligence (AGI), AI systems that are generally smarter than humans—and doing so in 'ways that benefit all of humanity.'

points.name: Chris Murphy
points.definition: AI that can reason more effectively than a human and problem solve better than humans in addition to writing, creating, coding, and driving
points.citation: First, once AI can reason more effectively than a human (called Artificial General Intelligence or "AGI"), AI will undoubtedly be a net job killer, not a job creator. Once a machine can problem solve better than a human - in addition to writing, creating, coding, and driving better than humans - the job displacement will be massive and devastating.

points.name: Chuck Schumer
points.definition: AGI definition is uncertain and lacks widespread agreement, requiring better definition in consultation with experts
points.citation: Recognizes the significant level of uncertainty and unknowns associated with general purpose AI systems achieving AGI. At the same time, the AI Working Group recognizes that there is not widespread agreement on the definition of AGI or threshold by which it will officially be achieved.

points.name: Cloudflare
points.definition: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is the term for a computer's ability to think original thoughts, reason, plan, and otherwise duplicate human cognitive abilities.
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is the term for a computer's ability to think original thoughts, reason, plan, and otherwise duplicate human cognitive abilities. In simpler terms, an AGI program would be, essentially, 'human' or 'conscious' in its intelligence.

points.name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
points.definition: AI systems capable of driving changes at least as significant as the Industrial Revolution
points.citation: Based in part on the rate of progress at the time, we estimated in 2016 that there was at least a 10% chance that transformative AI, by which we meant AI systems capable of driving changes at least as significant as the Industrial Revolution, could arrive within 20 years (i.e. by 2036).

points.name: Compassion in Machine Learning (CaML)
points.definition: CaML refers to AGI in the context of increasingly autonomous AI systems that make decisions and take actions on behalf of humans at scale, with particular concern about alignment and default values in such systems.
points.citation: AI agents are increasingly booking travel, managing schedules, and making purchasing decisions. As these systems scale, their default values get enacted millions of times. A travel agent that books a dolphin captivity show because it's the best keyword match isn't malicious, it's just indifferent. But indifference at scale is the problem. Models should take into account the impacts on non-humans just like they consider the impacts on humans. As AIs become increasingly autonomous and powerful, agentic benchmarks become increasingly important.

points.name: Conjecture
points.definition: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult
points.citation: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.

points.name: Connor Axiotes
points.definition: AGI systems with superhuman labour abilities, generality across all human tasks, and agency to plan and execute their own objectives.
points.citation: In order for the price of some labour to fall to zero, AI systems will have to reach a point of generality - meaning the AI can do any task that a human can do but to a superhuman level. Some call this artificial general intelligence (AGI).

points.name: Connor Leahy
points.definition: AGI is a system that when confronted with a protein solving a folding problem, invents Alpha fold
points.citation: For me, AGI is not a system that can do everything Alphafold can do, everything GPT can do, and everything DALLY can do. It's a system that when confronted with a protein solving a folding problem, invents Alpha fold.

points.name: ControlAI
points.definition: ControlAI defines artificial superintelligence as AI vastly smarter than humans that would be capable of replacing humans both individually and collectively as a species.
points.citation: At ControlAI, our mission is to prevent the risk of extinction posed by artificial superintelligence – AI vastly smarter than humans. This would be AI capable of replacing humans, both individually and collectively as a species.

points.name: Convergence Analysis
points.definition: Human-level AI capabilities that will emerge within 5 years, followed by intelligence beyond human capabilities
points.citation: The development of AI technology poses both great opportunities and perils for humanity. We're currently at a watershed moment in history - plausibly, human-level AI will be here within 5 years. Shortly after this, we'll be in a world powered by intelligence beyond human capabilities.

points.name: Craig Smith
points.definition: AGI requires systems with world models that understand underlying reality rather than just predicting text tokens based on probabilities from training data
points.citation: But it doesn't understand the underlying reality that that text describes. And a world model will. Or does and there's a lot of work that has to be done on them and a lot of money that has to be invested in scaling them. But to me that, that is a direction that personally I just think is much more fundamental and, and, and exciting in building true intelligence.

points.name: Dan Hendrycks
points.definition: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult
points.citation: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.

points.name: Dan Wang
points.definition: Wang defines AGI success not as achieving superintelligence but as successfully deploying AI technology across society for economic and practical benefit.
points.citation: Rather than "winning the AI race," I prefer to say that the US and China need to "win the AI future." There is no race with a clear end point or a shiny medal for first place. Winning the future is the more appropriately capacious term that incorporates the agenda to build good reasoning models as well as the effort to diffuse it across society.

points.name: Daniel Kokotajlo
points.definition: An AI system at least as good as the best human X'ers, for all cognitive tasks/skills/jobs X
points.citation: By AGI I mean "An AI system at least as good as the best human X'ers, for all cognitive tasks/skills/jobs X."

points.name: Daniel Susskind
points.definition: AGI as technologies that can do all tasks better than humans, contrasted with ANI (Artificial Narrow Intelligence) which are good only at specific tasks
points.citation: Then there is what computer scientists and the sort of people you're describing are really interested in which is Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) which are in technologies that can't just do one or two tasks, but they can do all tasks, and they can do them better than we can.

points.name: Daniela Amodei
points.definition: AGI as artificial intelligence that is as capable as a human, though she considers this concept outdated given AI's mixed performance across domains
points.citation: AGI is such a funny term because many years ago it was kind of a useful concept to say when will artificial intelligence be as capable as a human. I think maybe the construct itself is now wrong—or maybe not wrong, but just outdated.

points.name: Daniela Rus
points.definition: General intelligence as machines that can do everything a human can and more, distinguished from today's highly specialized AI systems that lack common sense, contextual understanding, and flexible reasoning.
points.citation: I think it's important to distinguish between general intelligence as we imagine it in science fiction – machines that can do everything a human can and more – and of what today's AI systems are capable.

points.name: Danielle Allen
points.definition: Technology that can meet and match humans on just about any task
points.citation: Not just an Age of AI, where machines and software, like Siri, perform specific and narrow tasks, but an Age of Artificial General Intelligence, in which technology can meet and match humans on just about any task.

points.name: Danielle Li
points.definition: AGI emphasizes broad reasoning and problem-solving capabilities
points.citation: While definitions of artificial general intelligence (AGI) emphasize broad reasoning and problem-solving capabilities (Bubeck et al., 2023), even highly capable models would still require context-specific training and examples to excel at actual workplace tasks (Mitchell, 2021).

points.name: Dario Amodei
points.definition: Powerful AI that is smarter than a Nobel Prize winner across most fields, can work autonomously for weeks, operates at 10x-100x human speed, and can be replicated in millions of instances - 'a country of geniuses in a datacenter'
points.citation: By powerful AI, I have in mind an AI model... In terms of pure intelligence, it is smarter than a Nobel Prize winner across most relevant fields – biology, programming, math, engineering, writing, etc.

points.name: Daron Acemoglu
points.definition: AI to perform essentially all human tasks
points.citation: Some experts believe that truly transformative implications, including artificial general intelligence (AGI) enabling AI to perform essentially all human tasks, could be around the corner.

points.name: David Autor
points.definition: AGI should not be defined as systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work, but rather as tools that enable new human capabilities rather than just replacing them
points.citation: Actually, I don't even like that definition of artificial general intelligence. The goal of machines should not be to just do what people do slightly better. Our tools are valuable to us because they allow us to do things we can't do.

points.name: David Duvenaud
points.definition: AGI defined as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult, grounded in Cattell-Horn-Carroll theory with ten core cognitive domains including reasoning, memory, and perception
points.citation: The lack of a concrete definition for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) obscures the gap between today's specialized AI and human-level cognition. This paper introduces a quantifiable framework to address this, defining AGI as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.

points.name: David Evan Harris
points.definition: AGI refers to a hypothetical form of AI with our level of intelligence, implying the ability to communicate effectively, make plans, learn new information, use reasoning to make decisions (including logic and problem-solving), and utilize these skills to achieve goals.
points.citation: Intelligence generally implies the ability to communicate effectively, make plans, learn new information, use reasoning to make decisions (including logic and problem-solving), and utilize these skills to achieve goals. AGI refers to a hypothetical form of AI with our level of intelligence.

points.name: David Krueger
points.definition: AGI is going to be an AI 'able to do anything a human can'
points.citation: OpenAI defines it as AI systems that are generally smarter than humans. But Evitable CEO David Scott Krueger puts it this way: AGI is going to be an AI "able to do anything a human can."

points.name: David Sacks
points.definition: AGI is a 'middle-to-middle' technology that augments human decisions rather than replacing them end-to-end, with AI models still at zero when it comes to setting their own objective functions.
points.citation: When it comes to setting their own objective functions, AI models are still at zero. Sacks adopts the wording of entrepreneur Balaji Srinivasan, who has described AGI as a 'middle-to-middle' technology that is powerful in augmenting human decisions, not replacing them end-to-end.

points.name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
points.definition: A theoretical form of advanced AI that would have capabilities that compare to or exceed humans across most economically valuable work.
points.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI): A theoretical form of advanced AI that would have capabilities that compare to or exceed humans across most economically valuable work.

points.name: Department of Defense
points.definition: A hypothetical stage in machine learning where an AI system can match or exceed human cognitive abilities across any task
points.citation: According to a description from IBM, artificial general intelligence, or AGI, is "a hypothetical stage in the development of machine learning(ML) in which an artificial intelligence (AI) system can match or exceed the cognitive abilities of human beings across any task."

points.name: Divya Siddarth
points.definition: Prefers moving from artificial general intelligence to augmented collective intelligence, where AI amplifies rather than replaces human agency.
points.citation: A better tech future moves from artificial general intelligence to augmented collective intelligence, where AI amplifies rather than replaces human agency.

points.name: Dustin Moskovitz
points.definition: Defines ASI (artificial superintelligence) as AI that is fully autonomous either for a particular role or for all roles at once.
points.citation: I think most of people who are tracking AI are waiting for this moment where it's fully autonomous either for a particular role or for all roles at once, it's how they define ASI [artificial superintelligence].

points.name: Dwarkesh Patel
points.definition: Can automate 95% of white collar work or do almost any job like 98% of jobs at least as well, fast, and cheaply as a human
points.citation: So the ultimate definition is can do almost any job like 98% of jobs at least as well fast uh cheaply as a human. I think the more um the definition that's often useful for near-term debates is can automate 95% of white color work because there's a clear path to get to that whereas robotics you know there's like a a long tale of things you have to do in the physical world and robotics is slower.

points.name: E. Glen Weyl
points.definition: Superintelligence already exists in collective systems humanity has created—markets, democracies, corporations, religious communities
points.citation: Glen Weyl, researcher and economist at Microsoft and co-founder of the Plurality Institute, does not think superintelligence is something we are waiting to build. He argues that it already exists—in the collective systems humanity has created, from markets and democracies to corporations and religious communities.

points.name: EA Long-Term Future Fund
points.definition: An AI system (or collection of systems) that can fully automate the vast majority (>90%) of roles in the 2025 economy, where a job is fully automatable when machines could be built to carry out the job better and more cheaply than human workers.
points.citation: We defined AGI as: An AI system (or collection of systems) that can fully automate the vast majority (>90%) of roles in the 2025 economy. A job is fully automatable when machines could be built to carry out the job better and more cheaply than human workers. Think feasibility, not adoption.

points.name: Edwin Chen
points.definition: Chen defines AGI in terms of achieving Nobel Prize-level capabilities across domains like poetry, mathematics, and scientific discovery.
points.citation: Edwin Chen thinks AI is capable of 'Nobel Prize-winning poetry, solving the Riemann hypothesis, and discovering the secrets of the universe'—but only if it's trained on data that captures human expertise, creativity, and values.

points.name: Eliezer Yudkowsky
points.definition: AGI is artificial intelligence that can autonomously research and develop AI without cognitive limitations compared to humans, particularly the ability to do autonomous research and overcome cognitive shortcomings independently.
points.citation: The timelines-relevant milestone of AGI is ability to autonomously research, especially AI's ability to develop AI that doesn't have particular cognitive limitations compared to humans.

points.name: Elon Musk
points.definition: A general purpose artificial intelligence system—a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human.
points.citation: His complaint describes it as "a general purpose artificial intelligence system—a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human."

points.name: Emily Bender
points.definition: AGI is a poorly defined, fuzzy concept that serves corporate interests rather than representing any specific technology
points.citation: Despite claims that machines may one day achieve an advanced level of 'general intelligence,' such a concept doesn't have an accepted definition.

points.name: Emmett Shear
points.definition: AGI requires open-ended continual learning and reflective theory of mind - the capacity to understand oneself and one's own learning process.
points.citation: If you have a general intelligence, it is necessarily capable of open ended continual learning. And the most difficult, challenging intellectual problem there is, reflective theory of mind. What do I know? You know, he knows that she knows. And literally to work on AGI is to work on the capacity at least for alignment and vice versa.

points.name: Epoch AI
points.definition: AI that can perform any cognitive task at the level of a skilled human
points.citation: What this doesn't allow is predicting when we have general intelligence, i.e. AI that can perform any cognitive task at the level of a skilled human.

points.name: Equilibria Network
points.definition: AGI is better understood as the emergent property of a distributed, multi-polar evolutionary process, not a discrete system to be built, boxed, and deployed.
points.citation: The dominant narrative of AGI frames it as a finish line: a discrete system to be built, boxed, and deployed. This view is fundamentally flawed. AGI is better understood as the emergent property of a distributed, multi-polar evolutionary process, driven by countless actors optimizing under local constraints.

points.name: Eric Schmidt
points.definition: AGI is the arrival of a new non-human intelligence, which is likely to have better reasoning skills than humans can have.
points.citation: So now we have the arrival of a new non-human intelligence, which is likely to have better reasoning skills than humans can have.

points.name: Erik Brynjolfsson
points.definition: AGI as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult
points.citation: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.

points.name: European AI Office
points.definition: AGI is a stage of AI development where AI would be capable to perform a wide range of cognitive tasks with similar or better results than humans
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is a broad concept referring to a stage of development of AI where AI would be capable to perform a wide range of cognitive tasks with similar or better results than humans.

points.name: European Artificial Intelligence Board
points.definition: AI capable of performing any cognitive task that humans can
points.citation: Current advances in memory, reasoning, and autonomous behaviour are paving the way towards Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), understood as AI capable of performing any cognitive task that humans can.

points.name: European Commission
points.definition: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is a broad concept referring to a stage of development of AI where AI would be capable to perform a wide range of cognitive tasks with similar or better results than humans.
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is a broad concept referring to a stage of development of AI where AI would be capable to perform a wide range of cognitive tasks with similar or better results than humans.

points.name: Existential Risk Observatory
points.definition: AGI is AI that is at least as capable as an average person for a wide range of cognitive tasks, historically measured by passing the Turing test.
points.citation: Later, this was equated with the concept of AGI: artificial general intelligence, or AI that is at least as capable as an average person for a wide range of cognitive tasks.) In other words, for decades, once the Turing test was passed, we would have AGI.

points.name: Exponential View
points.definition: Deliberately avoids using the term AGI because it's often unhelpful and distracting, preferring to focus on increasingly capable AI systems rather than a singular moment
points.citation: I deliberately avoid using the term 'artificial general intelligence' (AGI), not because it's irrelevant, but because it's often unhelpful and distracting, even when used by leading researchers.

points.name: Ezra Klein
points.definition: An A.I. system that can beat humans at almost any cognitive task
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence — an A.I. system that can beat humans at almost any cognitive task – is arriving in just a couple of years.

points.name: Federation of American Scientists
points.definition: AGI refers to a human-like ability to pursue long-term goals and exercise purposive behavior, seeking general cognitive abilities rather than being oriented around specific tasks.
points.citation: artificial general intelligence (AGI) — meaning a human-like ability to pursue long-term goals and exercise purposive behavior. Where AI is oriented around specific tasks, AGI seeks general cognitive abilities.

points.name: Fei-Fei Li
points.definition: AGI is an undefined concept that she doesn't know how to define, describing it as something people say 'you'll know when you see it' but she hasn't seen it.
points.citation: I frankly don't even know what A.G.I. means. People go, 'You'll know when you see it.' I guess I haven't seen it.

points.name: Foresight Institute
points.definition: the possible future one‐to‐one modeling of the function of the human brain
points.citation: In 2008, in a landmark WBE Roadmap report, Anders Sandberg defined WBE as 'the possible future one‐to‐one modeling of the function of the human brain.'

points.name: Forethought (AI Safety Research Nonprofit)
points.definition: AI that can substitute for human researchers and accelerate research to drive a century of technological progress over just a few years
points.citation: AI that can accelerate research could drive a century of technological progress over just a few years.

points.name: Founders Pledge
points.definition: Uses 'Advanced Artificial Intelligence' as effectively the same as 'Transformative AI' (TAI) but intentionally avoids being too prescriptive with definitions.
points.citation: I'm somewhat loose with definitions here, defining it effectively the same way as "Transformative AI" (TAI) but intentionally not being too prescriptive for the most part (except in cases where definitions become important).

points.name: Future of Life Institute
points.definition: AGI is characterized by the combination of Autonomy (independence of action), Generality (breadth of capability), and Intelligence (competence at tasks)
points.citation: Although AGI stands for "Artificial General Intelligence", it is a useful mnemonic to think of it as an "Autonomous General Intelligence": the triple combination of Autonomy (independence of action), Generality (breath of capability, application, and learning), and Intelligence (competence at tasks).

points.name: Gabriel Alfour
points.definition: A system that can alter the course of humanity, that can give you control of our entire earth, that can create weapons of mass destruction.
points.citation: So when we talk about super intelligence control and so on and so forth, we're talking about the control of a system that can alter the course of humanity, that can give you control of our entire earth, that can create weapons of mass destruction and so on and so forth.

points.name: Gary Marcus
points.definition: AGI is a shorthand for any intelligence that is flexible and general, with resourcefulness and reliability comparable to (or beyond) human intelligence
points.citation: I take AGI to be "a shorthand for any intelligence ... that is flexible and general, with resourcefulness and reliability comparable to (or beyond) human intelligence".

points.name: Geoffrey Hinton
points.definition: AI that is at least as good as humans at nearly all of the cognitive things that humans do
points.citation: I don't think there is agreement on what the term means. I use it to mean AI that is at least as good as humans at nearly all of the cognitive things that humans do.

points.name: Global AI Moratorium (GAIM)
points.definition: Defines focus as artificial superintelligence (ASI) systems rather than AGI specifically - systems that could surpass human intelligence and control
points.citation: Harry and Meghan join over two dozen prominent signatories supporting a statement organized by the Future of Life Institute that demands "a prohibition on the development of superintelligence" until specific safety conditions are met.

points.name: Global Catastrophic Risk Institute
points.definition: Systems with some generality, being able to field inquiries across a wide range of domains
points.citation: Current state-of-the-art systems already have some generality, being able to field inquiries across a wide range of domains, even while they still have significant limitations.

points.name: Global Partnership on AI
points.definition: AGI typically refers to AI systems that can perform a wide range of intellectual tasks at or above human level, with capabilities that generalize across domains, distinguished from current AI systems which excel in narrow domains but lack broader capabilities.
points.citation: While definitions vary, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) typically refers to AI systems that can perform a wide range of intellectual tasks at or above human level, with capabilities that generalize across domains.

points.name: Global Priorities Institute (University of Oxford)
points.definition: AI systems that are generally smarter than humans
points.citation: OpenAI states 'Our mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence—AI systems that are generally smarter than humans—benefits all of humanity.'

points.name: Good Ancestors
points.definition: AI models with human-like cognitive capabilities
points.citation: Markets forecast that Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) – AI models with human-like cognitive capabilities – will be developed during this term of government.

points.name: Google DeepMind
points.definition: AI that's at least as capable as humans at most cognitive tasks, integrated with agentic capabilities to understand, reason, plan, and execute actions autonomously
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence (AGI), AI that's at least as capable as humans at most cognitive tasks, could be here within the coming years.

points.name: Harvard Ash Center
points.definition: A nonbiological computational system that can perform any cognitive function currently performed by humans at the level of the median human or better
points.citation: Synthesising a vast literature, we can say that AGI would be a nonbiological computational system that can perform any cognitive function currently performed by humans at the level of the median human or better.

points.name: Helen Toner
points.definition: AGI is a fuzzy cloud of related concepts rather than a specific milestone, with jagged capabilities making different definitions fulfilled at different times
points.citation: These days, though, it's clear that AI capabilities are highly jagged. This makes it likely that there could be big gaps between when different definitions of AGI are fulfilled.

points.name: Henry Papadatos
points.definition: AGI traditionally meant machines that think, learn, and adapt like humans, but questions whether this anthropomorphic view makes sense given our limited understanding of human intelligence itself.
points.citation: The term AGI made sense from far away, when AI systems were very narrow. Now we have systems that are quite general already and the term does not mean as much.

points.name: Holden Karnofsky
points.definition: AGI represents potentially introducing the second advanced species ever - systems that can transform the world, make technology, and do the long list of things humans can do.
points.citation: The way I think of it is like, we are potentially about to introduce the second advanced species ever. There's one species ever that we know of — humans — that can transform the world, make its own technology, do any of a very long list of things. We're about to create the second one ever, and it will be the only one besides us.

points.name: Huawei
points.definition: Huawei defines AGI as human-level intelligence that can perform any intellectual task given necessary resources, requiring integration of AI with the physical world.
points.citation: AGI, sometimes called 'human-level AI' or 'strong AI,' typically refers to an artificial intelligence (AI) system capable of performing any task given the necessary resources.

points.name: IBM
points.definition: IBM defines AGI as a hypothetical stage where an artificial intelligence system can match or exceed the cognitive abilities of human beings across any task
points.citation: IBM gives one of the more succinct and straightforward definitions of AGI: 'Artificial general intelligence is a hypothetical stage in the development of machine learning in which an artificial intelligence system can match or exceed the cognitive abilities of human beings across any task'.

points.name: IIT Madras
points.definition: The vision of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), where machines can seamlessly learn and do anything that a human does.
points.citation: The quest for building machines that think and act like us has propelled significant advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI). While we now have systems that do restricted tasks very well, the vision of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), where machines can seamlessly learn and do anything that a human does, continues to elude us.

points.name: Ian Hogarth
points.definition: AGI is called 'God-like AI' because such technology could develop by itself and transform the world without supervision
points.citation: "A three-letter acronym doesn't capture the enormity of what AGI would represent, so I will refer to it as what is: God-like AI," Ian Hogarth wrote in the FT. Hogarth used that term, he said, because such technology could develop by itself and transform the world without supervision.

points.name: Ilya Sutskever
points.definition: A superintelligent learner that starts as a powerful learning system and becomes more capable through experience, like a 'superintelligent 15-year-old' rather than an omniscient oracle.
points.citation: Instead of imagining an all-knowing entity, think of AGI as: A superintelligent fifteen year old that can learn any job extremely fast. Not omniscient. Not preloaded with every skill. A learner that becomes smarter and more capable with experience.

points.name: Institute for Law & AI (LawAI)
points.definition: AI agents that can generally outperform humans in cognitive tasks and economically valuable activities
points.citation: The prospect of artificial superintelligence—AI agents that can generally outperform humans in cognitive tasks and economically valuable activities—will transform the legal order as we know it.

points.name: Jaan Tallinn
points.definition: AI that creates machines that are more and more intelligent, with the risk of humans ending up in a situation like gorillas - dominated by a smarter agent
points.citation: The thing about AI is, if you're creating machines that are more and more intelligent, you don't want to inadvertently end up in a situation that gorillas are these days, for example, that you have a smarter agent than you dominating the environment.

points.name: Jack Clark
points.definition: A single machine that can learn to do anything and everything better than humans - though Clark notes this sounds strange, it's precisely what companies are trying to build
points.citation: Though it sounds strange to the author, it's worth noting that building an 'everything machine' is precisely what a bunch of people are doing.

points.name: Jaime Sevilla
points.definition: AI systems that would, if widely available, result in societal changes comparable in magnitude to the industrial revolution, or AI that can substitute for humans in scientific endeavors.
points.citation: Another study attempts to predict when AI systems that would, if widely available, result in societal changes comparable in magnitude to the industrial revolution—their model estimated that such an outcome is 50% likely to occur by 2033.

points.name: Jakub Pachocki
points.definition: AI models that can deliver commercial results and conduct autonomous research
points.citation: Now, Pachocki sees the next major step as economic: AI models that can deliver commercial results and conduct autonomous research. "This for me is closest to what I previously emotionally thought of as AGI," he says.

points.name: Jan Leike
points.definition: AGI as systems capable of automated AI/ML research that can match or exceed human researchers
points.citation: Our overall goal is to get to a point where we can automate alignment research. And what this doesn't mean is we're not trying to train a system that's really good at ML research, or that is really smart or something.

points.name: Jared Kaplan
points.definition: AGI conceptualized on two axes: what environments can an AI actually go out and act in, and just how complex are the things that AI can do - like can it do something that would take me a minute or 10 minutes or an hour or a day.
points.citation: I think of it on two axes. There's what environments can an AI actually go out and act in... and just how complex are the things that AI can do. Like can it do something that would take me a minute or 10 minutes or an hour or a day?

points.name: Jay Obernolte
points.definition: An AI algorithm that is smarter than a human that doesn't want to be turned off, though he notes it depends on how you define AGI and intelligence, and expresses skepticism about current paths to AGI
points.citation: I actually think I I'm kind of an AGI skeptic. I think that if you, it depends on how you define AGI, like if you define AGI, well, what does it mean to be smarter than a human? Well, if you can pass all these tests, if you can pass a, uh, you know, if you can pass the bar in California and you can pass, uh, a test to get your medical license in Vermont, you know, then that's pretty smart.

points.name: Jensen Huang
points.definition: AGI defined as an AI system able to start, grow, and run a successful technology company worth more than $1 billion
points.citation: Fridman, the podcast's host, defines AGI as an AI system that's able to 'essentially do your job,' as in start, grow, and run a successful tech company worth more than $1 billion. He then asks Huang when he believes AGI will be real — asking if it's, say, five, 10, 15, or 20 years away — and Huang responds, 'I think it's now. I think we've achieved AGI.'

points.name: John Schulman
points.definition: AI that can match or exceed human abilities in basically every area
points.citation: You could define it as AI that can match or exceed human abilities in basically every area.

points.name: John Tasioulas
points.definition: Intelligence that replicates the entire spectrum of human intelligence, able to write a poem, make a cancer diagnosis, make a hiring decision, do everything that a human can do
points.citation: Artificial general intelligence is intelligence that is replicating the entire spectrum of human intelligence. So able to write a poem, make a cancer diagnosis, make a hiring decision, do everything that a human can do.

points.name: Jon Husted
points.definition: AI as the most powerful assistive technology rather than autonomous general intelligence
points.citation: So what is it that's happening right now that can differentiate us? It's AI, because AI is the most powerful assistive technology in my lifetime — probably in the history of the world.

points.name: Joni Ernst
points.definition: Ernst views AI as a tool for making government more efficient across agencies and departments.
points.citation: We all think of DOGE as slashing the waste in federal government, but we can also use it and we forget, Department of Government Efficiency, AI can make us so much more efficient across every space and every agency across our federal government.

points.name: Joseph Stiglitz
points.definition: AI as 'IA' for intelligence assisting - a tool that supplements human capabilities rather than replacing them, like a microscope or telescope that augments our abilities.
points.citation: IA is intelligence assisting. I gave the analogy of the microscope and telescope—it sort of made our eyes see things that we couldn't otherwise see. So they augmented our capabilities.

points.name: Joshua Gans
points.definition: Recent advances in AI are exclusively advances in statistical techniques for prediction that enhance decision-making
points.citation: It is well-recognized that recent advances in AI are exclusively advances in statistical techniques for prediction. While this may facilitate automation, this result is secondary to AI's impact on decision-making.

points.name: Karen Hao
points.definition: AGI is an ever-present goalpost that keeps shifting, depending on where the company wants to go, with OpenAI having multiple definitions internally.
points.citation: So it's kind of just this ever-present goalpost that keeps shifting, depending on where the company wants to go. You know, they have a full range, a variety of different definitions that they've used throughout the years. In fact, they even have a joke internally: If you ask 13 OpenAI researchers what AGI is, you'll get 15 definitions.

points.name: Kartik Hosanagar
points.definition: AGI should not be LLMs overloaded as monolithic systems, but rather LLMs acting as language processors that interface with other computational systems to generate reliable results.
points.citation: But LLMs are being overloaded as monolithic AGI system—one that undeniably excels at language but thrust with responsibilities far beyond their capabilities.

points.name: Kevin Kelly
points.definition: Kelly defines intelligence as having three modes: Knowledge reasoning (LLM-type), World sense (spatial/physical intelligence), and Continuous learning - with current AI only having the first
points.citation: I propose three general classes of cognition that together can make something like a human intelligence. The three modes are: 1) Knowledge reasoning, 2) World sense, and 3) Continuous memory and learning.

points.name: Kevin Roose
points.definition: AGI can mean anything from 'AI that can do some office jobs' to 'the godlike shoggoth that will enslave and/or destroy humanity' - it's become an organizing idea for the AI industry
points.citation: The working title is 'The AGI Chronicles,' less because I love the term AGI — which can mean anything these days from 'AI that can do some office jobs' to 'the godlike shoggoth that will enslave and/or destroy humanity' — than because AGI has become an organizing idea for the AI industry, a rallying cry, an ideological Schelling point.

points.name: Khosla Ventures
points.definition: AI that amplifies and multiplies the human brain, much like steam engines once amplified muscle power, offering a near-infinite expansion of brainpower with technology capabilities that far exceed human intelligence.
points.citation: AI amplifies and multiplies the human brain, much like steam engines once amplified muscle power. The pace of change will be hard to predict or anticipate, especially as technology capabilities far exceed human intelligence and penetrate society at varying rates.

points.name: LawZero
points.definition: AGI is defined as AI systems that approach or surpass human levels of competence across many cognitive abilities, particularly including capacity for agency and goal-directed behavior.
points.citation: As we approach or surpass human levels of competence across many cognitive abilities, is it still wise to imitate humans along with their cognitive biases, moral weaknesses, and potential for deception, biases and untrustworthiness? Is it reasonable to train AI that will be more and more agentic while we do not understand their potentially catastrophic consequences?

points.name: Leopold Aschenbrenner
points.definition: Models as smart as PhDs or experts that can work beside us as coworkers, and can automate AI research itself
points.citation: Another jump like that [from GPT-2 to GPT-4] very well could take us to AGI, to models as smart as PhDs or experts that can work beside us as coworkers. Perhaps most importantly, if these AI systems could automate AI research itself, that would set in motion intense feedback loops…

points.name: Liang Wenfeng
points.definition: AGI represents the ultimate form of AI development capable of cross-domain learning and reasoning abilities, distinguished from specialized artificial intelligence.
points.citation: Liang points out that while generative AI has widespread applications today, it fundamentally belongs to specialized artificial intelligence categories; whereas AGI represents the ultimate form of AI development capable of cross-domain learning and reasoning abilities.

points.name: Lila Ibrahim
points.definition: AGI is versatile AI that equals humans in all cognitive abilities and can act as a personal assistant with broad world understanding, context awareness, and memory.
points.citation: Aber wir wollen wirklich vielseitige KI bauen, die in allen kognitiven Fähigkeiten dem Menschen ebenbürtig ist. Sie muss ein breites Verständnis der Welt haben, den Kontext des Nutzers mit einbeziehen und ein Gedächtnis haben.

points.name: MIT Algorithmic Alignment Group
points.definition: AGI describes the potential of artificial intelligence to match or surpass human cognitive capabilities
points.citation: AGI describes the potential of artificial intelligence to match or surpass human cognitive capabilities.

points.name: MIT CSAIL
points.definition: Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines
points.citation: The broad goal of U.S. AI policy should be to drive toward what we call Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines.

points.name: MIT Computer Science & Artificial Intelligence Laboratory
points.definition: A level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines
points.citation: The broad goal of U.S. AI policy should be to drive toward what we call Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines.

points.name: MIT FutureTech
points.definition: Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, processing and pattern toward deep reasoning, and autonomous solving across disciplines.
points.citation: The broad goal of U.S. AI policy should be to drive toward what we call Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, processing and pattern toward deep reasoning, and autonomous solving across disciplines.

points.name: MIT Media Lab
points.definition: General Artificial Intelligence (AGI) refers to AI systems with human-like versatility, capable of performing a wide range of tasks across various domains with adaptability and reasoning.
points.citation: General Artificial Intelligence (AGI) refers to AI systems with human-like versatility, capable of performing a wide range of tasks across various domains with adaptability and reasoning.

points.name: Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
points.definition: AI that substantially surpasses humans in all capacities, including economic, scientific, and military ones
points.citation: The development of systems with human-level generality is likely to quickly result in artificial superintelligence (ASI): AI that substantially surpasses humans in all capacities, including economic, scientific, and military ones.

points.name: Marc Andreessen
points.definition: AI systems that are capable of matching human-level performance across a wide range of intellectual tasks
points.citation: AGI, or artificial general intelligence, refers to AI systems that are capable of matching human-level performance across a wide range of intellectual tasks.

points.name: Marc Benioff
points.definition: AI can perform complex, human-level tasks
points.citation: Whether AI will reach 'AGI' —an ill-defined milestone generally understood to be when AI can perform complex, human-level tasks —its CEO is less confident.

points.name: Marco Rubio
points.definition: AI is simply the automated statistical analysis of data that uses what happened yesterday to predict what will happen today or tomorrow
points.citation: First, we know what AI is. It's not an alien consciousness, nor is it a magic eight-ball with the solution to every world problem at its fingertips. AI is simply the automated statistical analysis of data. Think of it as a computer program that uses what happened yesterday to predict what will happen today or tomorrow.

points.name: Margaret Mitchell
points.definition: Mitchell views AGI as an ambiguous concept at best that poses risks - essentially 'just vibes and snake oil' rather than a coherent technical goal
points.citation: One of the most influential narratives around the promise of AI is that, one day, we will be able to build artificial general intelligence (AGI) systems that are at least as capable or intelligent as people. But the concept is ambiguous at best and poses many risks, argues Mitchell.

points.name: Margrethe Vestager
points.definition: General purpose artificial intelligence that challenges humanity through its broad capabilities and lack of defined purpose
points.citation: In her speech, Vestager said that large digital platforms are 'challenging democracy,' but that 'general purpose artificial intelligence' is 'challenging humanity.'

points.name: Mariana Mazzucato
points.definition: AI is not a sector - it's a general purpose technology that is and will continue to shape all sectors of our economy.
points.citation: AI is not a sector - it's a general purpose technology that is and will continue to shape all sectors of our economy.

points.name: Marius Hobbhahn
points.definition: AI systems that can automate AI research itself and can replace a top researcher at a frontier AI company
points.citation: Step 1: build AGI (for the purpose of this post, AGI refers to an AI system that can replace a top researcher at a frontier AI company)

points.name: Mark Zuckerberg
points.definition: Intelligence with breadth across different capabilities including reasoning and intuition, rather than human-level or superhuman intelligence
points.citation: I don't have a one-sentence, pithy definition. You can quibble about if general intelligence is akin to human level intelligence, or is it like human-plus, or is it some far-future super intelligence. But to me, the important part is actually the breadth of it, which is that intelligence has all these different capabilities where you have to be able to reason and have intuition.

points.name: Martin Casado
points.definition: AGI requires two key capabilities: plasticity (the ability to keep learning after training without catastrophic forgetting) and causal modeling (moving from pattern matching/correlation to understanding cause and effect).
points.citation: To get to AGI, I think there are two things that need to happen. One is this plasticity... Secondly, we have to move from correlation to causation.

points.name: Masayoshi Son
points.definition: AGI is equivalent to human brain intelligence, while ASI is 10,000 times smarter than the human brain
points.citation: The definition of AGI is the same as the human brain, while the definition of ASI—how super is it? 10 times super or 100 times super? My definition of ASI is 10,000 times smarter than the human brain

points.name: Max Tegmark
points.definition: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.
points.citation: AGI is an AI that can match or exceed the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.

points.name: Meredith Whittaker
points.definition: AGI is a marketing term and religious concept, not a technical term of art
points.citation: AGI is a marketing term. It is over 70 years old and over its long history, it has been applied to a vast and disparate variety of technical methods. It is more a signifier of aspiration that I would like to create something that I might view as humanlike or as behaving in a human-like way.

points.name: Meta AI
points.definition: AGI involves breadth of intelligence capabilities including reasoning and intuition, rather than a specific threshold, with arrival being a gradual process.
points.citation: I don't have a one-sentence, pithy definition. You can quibble about if general intelligence is akin to human level intelligence, or is it like human-plus, or is it some far-future super intelligence. But to me, the important part is actually the breadth of it, which is that intelligence has all these different capabilities where you have to be able to reason and have intuition.

points.name: Metaculus
points.definition: AGI requires passing a hard Turing test, having general robotic capabilities, achieving 75% accuracy on MMLU benchmark tasks with 90% mean accuracy, and achieving 90% accuracy on APPS coding benchmark.
points.citation: The AI system needs to be able to: Pass a really hard Turing test. Have general robotic capabilities (being able to assemble a "circa-2021 Ferrari 312 T4 1:8 scale automobile model" or equivalent). Achieve "at least 75% accuracy in every task and 90% mean accuracy across all tasks" on the MMLU benchmark, which measures expertise in a wide range of academic subjects. Achieve at least 90% accuracy with a single attempt for each question on the APPS benchmark, which measures coding skills.

points.name: Mira Murati
points.definition: Highly autonomous systems that are capable of producing economic output, significant economic output - systems that can generalize across different domains with human-level capability
points.citation: By artificial general intelligence, we usually mean highly autonomous systems that are capable of producing economic output, significant economic output. In other words, systems that can generalize across different domains. It's human-level capability.

points.name: Mistral AI
points.definition: AGI is "too simple of a concept" - "There's no such thing as one system that is going to be solving all of the problems of the world... It's never going to exist." Enterprises need specialized systems, not a single AGI abstraction.
points.citation: There's no such thing as one system that is going to be solving all of the problems of the world... It's never going to exist. Just like you don't have any human that is able to solve every task in the world, you of course have to have some level of specialization to actually solve problems.

points.name: Modeling Cooperation
points.definition: AGI emerging from LLM development will be a distillation of human thought that inherently embodies cooperative structures rather than an alien optimizer with orthogonal goals.
points.citation: If it does emerge from the current trajectory of large language model development it will not be an alien optimiser. It will be something far stranger and, this essay will argue, far less dangerous: a distillation of the entire corpus of human thought, pressed into coherence by mathematical operations that select for the same structural properties that biological evolution selects for in successful organisms. Cooperation. Mutualism.

points.name: Mozilla Foundation
